Rohanne at least had touched him; he had the swollen lip to prove it. Don't be daft. She's not for the likes of you. She is too small, too clever, and much too dangerous.
23	
24	Drowsing at long last, Dunk dreamed. He was running through a glade in the heart of Wat's Wood, running toward Rohanne, and she was shooting arrows at him. Each shaft she loosed flew true, and pierced him through the chest, yet the pain was strangely sweet. He should have turned and fled, but he ran toward her instead, running slowly as you always did in dreams, as if the very air had turned to honey. Another arrow came, and yet another. Her quiver seemed to have no end of shafts. Her eyes were gray and green and full of mischief. Your gown brings out the color of your eyes, he meant to say to her, but she was not wearing any gown, or any clothes at all. Across her small breasts was a faint spray of freckles, and her nipples were red and hard as little berries. The arrows made him look like some great porcupine as he went stumbling to her feet, but somehow he still found the strength to grab her braid. With one hard yank he pulled her down on top of him and kissed her.
25	
26	He woke suddenly, at the sound of a shout.
27	
28	In the darkened cellar, all was confusion. Curses and complaints echoed back and forth, and men were stumbling over one another as they fumbled for their spears or breeches. No one knew what was happening. Egg found the tallow candle and got it lit, to shed some light upon the scene. Dunk was the first one up the steps. He almost collided with Sam Stoops rushing down, puffing like a bellows and babbling incoherently. Dunk had to hold him by both shoulders to keep him from falling. "Sam, what's wrong?"
29	
30	"The sky," the old man whimpered. "The sky !" No more sense could be gotten from him, so they all went up to the roof for a look. Ser Eustace was there before them, standing by the parapets in his bedrobe, staring off into the distance.
31	
32	The sun was rising in the west.
33	
34	It was a long moment before Dunk realized what that meant. "Wat's Wood is afire," he said in a hushed voice. From down at the base of the tower came the sound of Bennis cursing, a stream of such surpassing filth that it might have made Aegon the Unworthy blush. Sam Stoops began to pray.
35	
36	They were too far away to make out flames, but the red glow engulfed half the western horizon, and above the light the stars were vanishing. The King's Crown was half gone already, obscured behind a veil of the rising smoke.
37	
38	Fire and sword, she said.
39	
40	The fire burned until morning. No one in Standfast slept that night. Before long they could smell the smoke, and see flames dancing in the distance like girls in scarlet skirts. They all wondered if the fire would engulf them. Dunk stood behind the parapets, his eyes burning, watching for riders in the night.
41	
42	"Bennis," he said, when the brown knight came up, chewing on his sourleaf, "it's you she wants. Might be you should go."
43	
44	"What, run?" he brayed. "On my horse? Might as well try to fly off on one o' these damned chickens."
45	
46	"Then give yourself up. She'll only slit your nose."
47	
48	"I like my nose how it is, lunk. Let her try and take me, we'll see what gets slit open." He sat cross-legged with his back against a merlon and took a whetstone from his pouch to sharpen his sword.
49	
50	Ser Eustace stood above him. In low voices, they spoke of how to fight the war. "The Longinch will expect us at the dam," Dunk heard the old knight say, "so we will burn her crops instead. Fire for fire."
51	
52	Ser Bennis thought that would be just the thing, only maybe they should put her mill to the torch as well.
53	
54	"It's six leagues on t'other side o' the castle, the Longinch won't be looking for us there. Burn the mill and kill the miller, that'll cost her dear."
55	
56	Egg was listening, too. He coughed, and looked at Dunk with wide white eyes. "Ser, you have to stop them."
57	
58	"How?" Dunk asked. The Red Widow will stop them. Her, and that Lucas the Longinch. "They're only making noise, Egg. It's that, or piss their breeches. And it's naught to do with us now."
59	
60	Dawn came with hazy gray skies and air that burned the eyes. Dunk meant to make an early start, though after their sleepless night he did not know how far they'd get. He and Egg broke their fast on boiled eggs while Bennis was rousting the others outside for more drill. They are Osgrey men and we are not, he told himself. He ate four of the eggs. Ser Eustace owed him that much, as he saw it. Egg ate two. They washed them down with ale.
61	
62	"We could go to Fair Isle, ser," the boy said as they were gathering up their things. "If they're being raided by the ironmen, Lord Farman might be looking for some swords."
63	
64	It was a good thought. "Have you ever been to Fair Isle?"
65	
66	"No, ser," Egg said, "but they say it's fair. Lord Farman's seat is fair, too. It's called Faircastle."
67	
68	Dunk laughed. "Faircastle it shall be." He felt as if a great weight had been lifted off his shoulders. "I'll see to the horses," he said, when he'd tied his armor up in a bundle, secured with hempen rope. "Go to the roof and get our bedrolls, squire." The last thing he wanted this morning was another confrontation with the chequy lion. "If you see Ser Eustace, let him be."
69	
70	"I will, ser."
71	
72	Outside, Bennis had his recruits lined up with their spears and shields, and was trying to teach them to advance in unison. The brown knight paid Dunk not the slightest heed as he crossed the yard. He will lead the whole lot of them to death. The Red Widow could be here any moment. Egg came bursting from the tower door and clattered down the wooden steps with their bedrolls. Above him, Ser Eustace stood stiffly on the balcony, his hands resting on the parapet. When his eyes met Dunk's his mustache quivered, and he quickly turned away. The air was hazy with blowing smoke.
73	
74	Bennis had his shield slung across his back, a tall kite shield of unpainted wood, dark with countless layers of old varnish and girded all about with iron. It bore no blazon, only a center bosse that reminded Dunk of some great eye, shut tight. As blind as he is. "How do you mean to fight her?" Dunk asked.
75	
76	Ser Bennis looked at his soldiers, his mouth running red with sourleaf. "Can't hold the hill with so few spears. Got to be the tower. We all hole up inside." He nodded at the door. "Only one way in. Haul up them wooden steps, and there's no way they can reach us."
77	
78	"Until they build some steps of their own. They might bring ropes and grapnels, too, and swarm down on you through the roof. Unless they just stand back with their crossbows and fill you full of quarrels while you're trying to hold the door."
79	
80	The Melons, Beans, and Barleycorns were listening to all they said. All their brave talk had blown away, though there was no breath of wind. They stood clutching their sharpened sticks, looking at Dunk and Bennis and each other.
81	
82	"This lot won't do you a lick of good," Dunk said, with a nod at the ragged Osgrey army. "The Red Widow's knights will cut them to pieces if you leave them in the open, and their spears won't be any use inside that tower."
83	
84	"They can chuck things off the roof," said Bennis. "Treb is good at chucking rocks."
85	
86	"He could chuck a rock or two, I suppose," said Dunk, "until one of the Widow's crossbowmen puts a bolt through him."
87	
88	"Ser?" Egg stood beside him. "Ser, if we mean to go, we'd best be gone, in case the Widow comes."
89	
90	The boy was right. If we linger, we'll be trapped here. Yet still Dunk hesitated. "Let them go, Bennis."
91	
92	"What, lose our valiant lads?" Bennis looked at the peasants, and brayed laughter. "Don't you lot be getting any notions," he warned them. "I'll gut any man who tries to run."
93	
94	"Try, and I'll gut you." Dunk drew his sword. "Go home, all of you," he told the smallfolk. "Go back to your villages, and see if the fire's spared your homes and crops."
95	
96	No one moved. The brown knight stared at him, his mouth working. Dunk ignored him. "Go," he told the smallfolk once again. It was as if some god had put the word into his mouth. Not the Warrior. Is there a god for fools? "GO!" he said again, roaring it this time. "Take your spears and shields, but go , or you won't live to see the morrow. Do you want to kiss your wives again? Do you want to hold your children? Go home! Have you all gone deaf?"
97	
98	They hadn't. A mad scramble ensued amongst the chickens. Big Rob trod on a hen as he made his dash, and Pate came within half a foot of disemboweling Will Bean when his own spear tripped him up, but off they went, running. The Melons went one way, the Beans another, the Barleycorns a third. Ser Eustace was shouting down at them from above, but no one paid him any mind. They are deaf to him at least, Dunk thought.
99	
100	By the time the old knight emerged from his tower and came scrambling down the steps, only Dunk and Egg and Bennis remained among the chickens. "Come back," Ser Eustace shouted at his fast-fleeing host. "You do not have my leave to go. You do not have my leave!"
101	
102	"No use, m'lord," said Bennis. "They're gone."
103	
104	Ser Eustace rounded on Dunk, his mustache quivering with rage. "You had no right to send them away. No right! I told them not to go, I forbade it. I forbade you to dismiss them."
105	
106	"We never heard you, my lord." Egg took off his hat to fan away the smoke. "The chickens were cackling too loud."
107	
108	The old man sank down onto Standfast's lowest step. "What did that woman offer you to deliver me to her?" he asked Dunk in a bleak voice. "How much gold did she give you to betray me, to send my lads away and leave me here alone?"
109	
110	"You're not alone, m'lord." Dunk sheathed his sword. "I slept beneath your roof, and ate your eggs this morning. I owe you some service still. I won't go slinking off with my tail between my legs. My sword's still here." He touched the hilt.
111	
112	"One sword." The old knight got slowly to his feet. "What can one sword hope to do against that woman?"
113	
114	"Try and keep her off your land, to start with." Dunk wished he were as certain as he sounded.
115	
116	The old knight's mustache trembled every time he took a breath. "Yes," he said at last. "Better to go boldly than hide behind stone walls. Better to die a lion than a rabbit. We were the Marshalls of the Northmarch for a thousand years. I must have my armor." He started up the steps.
117	
118	Egg was looking up at Dunk. "I never knew you had a tail, ser," the boy said.
119	
120	"Do you want a clout in the ear?"
121	
122	"No, ser. Do you want your armor?"
123	
124	"That," Dunk said, "and one thing more."
125	
126	There was talk of Ser Bennis coming with them, but in the end Ser Eustace commanded him to stay and hold the tower. His sword would be of little use against the odds that they were like to face, and the sight of him would inflame the Widow further.
127	
128	The brown knight did not require much convincing. Dunk helped him knock loose the iron pegs that held the upper steps in place. Bennis clambered up them, untied the old gray hempen rope, and hauled on it with all his strength. Creaking and groaning, the wooden stair swung upward, leaving ten feet of air between the top stone step and the tower's only entrance. Sam Stoops and his wife were both inside.
129	
130	The chickens would need to fend for themselves. Sitting below on his gray gelding, Ser Eustace called up to say, "If we have not returned by nightfall . . ."
131	
132	". . . I'll ride for Highgarden, m'lord, and tell Lord Tyrell how that woman burned your wood and murdered you."
133	
134	Dunk followed Egg and Maester down the hill. The old man came after, his armor rattling softly. For once a wind was rising, and he could hear the flapping of his cloak.
135	
136	Where Wat's Wood had stood they found a smoking wasteland. The fire had largely burned itself out by the time they reached the wood, but here and there a few patches were still burning, fiery islands in a sea of ash and cinders. Elsewhere the trunks of burned trees thrust like blackened spears into the sky. Other trees had fallen and lay athwart the west way with limbs charred and broken, dull red fires smoldering inside their hollow hearts. There were hot spots on the forest floor as well, and places where the smoke hung in the air like a hot gray haze. Ser Eustace was stricken with a fit of coughing, and for a few moments Dunk feared the old man would need to turn back, but finally it passed.
137	
138	They rode past the carcass of a red deer, and later on what might have been a badger. Nothing lived, except the flies. Flies could live through anything, it seemed.
139	
140	"The Field of Fire must have looked like this," Ser Eustace said. "It was there our woes began, two hundred years ago. The last of the green kings perished on that field, with the finest flowers of the Reach around him. My father said the dragonfire burned so hot that their swords melted in their hands.
141	
142	Afterward the blades were gathered up, and went to make the Iron Throne. Highgarden passed from kings to stewards, and the Osgreys dwindled and diminished, until the Marshalls of the Northmarch were no more than landed knights bound in fealty to the Rowans."
143	
144	Dunk had nothing to say to that, so they rode in silence for a time, till Ser Eustace coughed, and said,
145	
146	"Ser Duncan, do you remember the story that I told you?"
147	
148	"I might, ser," said Dunk. "Which one?"
149	
150	"The Little Lion."
151	
152	"I remember. He was the youngest of five sons."
153	
154	"Good." He coughed again. "When he slew Lancel Lannister, the westermen turned back. Without the king there was no war. Do you understand what I am saying?"
155	
156	"Aye," Dunk said reluctantly. Could I kill a woman? For once Dunk wished he were as thick as that castle wall. It must not come to that. I must not let it come to that.
157	
158	A few green trees still stood where the west way crossed the Chequy Water. Their trunks were charred and blackened on one side. Just beyond, the water glimmered darkly. Blue and green, Dunk thought, but all the gold is gone. The smoke had veiled the sun.
159	
160	Ser Eustace halted when he reached the water's edge. "I took a holy vow. I will not cross that stream. Not so long as the land beyond is hers ." The old knight wore mail and plate beneath his yellowed surcoat. His sword was on his hip.
161	
162	"What if she never comes, ser?" Egg asked.
163	
164	With fire and sword, Dunk thought. "She'll come."
165	
166	She did, and within the hour. They heard her horses first, and then the faint metallic sound of clinking armor, growing louder. The drifting smoke made it hard to tell how far off they were, until her banner bearer pushed through the ragged gray curtain. His staff was crowned by an iron spider painted white and red, with the black banner of the Webbers hanging listlessly beneath. When he saw them across the water, he halted on the bank. Ser Lucas Inchfield appeared half a heartbeat later, armored head to heel.
167	
168	Only then did Lady Rohanne herself appear, astride a coal-black mare decked out in strands of silverly silk, like unto a spider's web. The Widow's cloak was made of the same stuff. It billowed from her shoulders and her wrists, as light as air. She was armored, too, in a suit of green enamel scale chased with gold and silver. It fit her figure like a glove, and made her look as if she were garbed in summer leaves. Her long red braid hung down behind her, bouncing as she rode. Septon Sefton rode red-faced at her side, atop a big gray gelding. On her other side was her young maester, Cerrick, mounted on a mule.
169	
170	More knights came after, half a dozen of them, attended by as many esquires. A column of mounted crossbowmen brought up the rear, and fanned out to either side of the road when they reached the Chequy Water and saw Dunk waiting on the other side. There were three-and-thirty fighting men all told, excluding the septon, the maester, and the Widow herself. One of the knights caught Dunk's eye; a squat bald keg of a man in mail and leather, with an angry face and an ugly goiter on his neck.
171	
172	The Red Widow walked her mare to the edge of the water. "Ser Eustace, Ser Duncan," she called across the stream, "we saw your fire burning in the night."
173	
174	"Saw it?" Ser Eustace shouted back. "Aye, you saw it . . . after you made it."
175	
176	"That is a vile accusation."
177	
178	"For a vile act."
179	
180	"I was asleep in my bed last night, with my ladies all around me. The shouts from the walls awoke me, as they did almost everyone. Old men climbed up steep tower steps to look, and babes at the breast saw the red light and wept in fear. And that is all I know of your fire, ser."
181	
182	"It was your fire, woman," insisted Ser Eustace. "My wood is gone. Gone, I say!"
183	
184	Septon Sefton cleared his throat. "Ser Eustace," he boomed, "there are fires in the kingswood too, and even in the rainwood. The drought has turned all our woods to kindling."
185	
186	Lady Rohanne raised an arm and pointed. "Look at my fields, Osgrey. How dry they are. I would have been a fool to set a fire. Had the wind changed direction, the flames might well have leapt the stream, and burned out half my crops."
187	
188	"Might have?" Ser Eustace shouted. "It was my woods that burned, and you that burned them. Most like you cast some witch's spell to drive the wind, just as you used your dark arts to slay your husbands and your brothers!"
189	
190	Lady Rohanne's face grew harder. Dunk had seen that look at Coldmoat, just before she slapped him.
191	
192	"Prattle," she told the old man. "I will waste no more words on you, ser. Produce Bennis of the Brown Shield, or we will come and take him."
193	
194	"That you shall not do," Ser Eustace declared in ringing tones. "That you shall never do." His mustache twitched. "Come no farther. This side of the stream is mine, and you are not wanted here. You shall have no hospitality from me. No bread and salt, not even shade and water. You come as an intruder. I forbid you to set foot on Osgrey land."
195	
196	Lady Rohanne drew her braid over her shoulder. "Ser Lucas," was all she said. The Longinch made a gesture, the crossbowmen dismounted, winched back their bowstrings with the help of hook and stirrup, and plucked quarrels from their quivers. "Now, ser," her ladyship called out, when every bow was nocked and raised and ready, "what was it you forbade me?"
197	
198	Dunk had heard enough. "If you cross the stream without leave, you are breaking the king's peace."
199	
200	Septon Sefton urged his horse forward a step. "The king will neither know nor care," he called. "We are all the Mother's children, ser. For her sake, stand aside."
201	
202	Dunk frowned. "I don't know much of gods, septon . . . but aren't we the Warrior's children, too?" He rubbed the back of his neck. "If you try to cross, I'll stop you."
203	
204	Ser Lucas the Longinch laughed. "Here's a hedge knight who yearns to be a hedgehog, my lady," he said to the Red Widow. "Say the word, and we'll put a dozen quarrels in him. At this distance they will punch through that armor like it was made of spit."
205	
206	"No. Not yet, ser." Lady Rohanne studied him from across the stream. "You are two men and a boy. We are three-and-thirty. How do you propose to stop us crossing?"
207	
208	"Well," said Dunk, "I'll tell you. But only you."
209	
210	"As you wish." She pressed her heels into her horse and rode her out into the stream. When the water reached the mare's belly, she halted, waiting. "Here I am. Come closer, ser. I promise not to sew you in a sack."
211	
212	Ser Eustace grasped Dunk by the arm before he could respond. "Go to her," the old knight said, "but remember the Little Lion."
213	
214	"As you say, m'lord." Dunk walked Thunder down into the water. He drew up beside her and said,
215	
216	"M'lady."
217	
218	"Ser Duncan." She reached up and laid two fingers on his swollen lip. "Did I do this, ser?"
219	
220	"No one else has slapped my face of late, m'lady."
221	
222	"That was bad of me. A breach of hospitality. The good septon has been scolding me." She gazed across the water at Ser Eustace. "I scarce remember Addam any longer. It was more than half my life ago. I remember that I loved him, though. I have not loved any of the others."
223	
224	"His father put him in the blackberries, with his brothers," Dunk said. "He was fond of blackberries."
225	
226	"I remember. He used to pick them for me, and we'd eat them in a bowl of cream."
227	
228	"The king pardoned the old man for Daemon," said Dunk. "It is past time you pardoned him for Addam."
229	
230	"Give me Bennis, and I'll consider that."
231	
232	"Bennis is not mine to give."
233	
234	She sighed. "I would as like not have to kill you."
235	
236	"I would as like not die."
237	
238	"Then give me Bennis. We'll cut his nose off and hand him back, and that will be the end of that."
239	
240	"It won't, though," Dunk said. "There's still the dam to deal with, and the fire. Will you give us the men who set it?"
241	
242	"There were lantern bugs in that wood," she said. "It may be they set the fire off, with their little lanterns."
243	
244	"No more teasing now, m'lady," Dunk warned her. "This is no time for it. Tear down the dam, and let Ser Eustace have the water to make up for the wood. That's fair, is it not?"
245	
246	"It might be, if I had burned the wood. Which I did not. I was at Coldmoat, safe abed." She looked down at the water. "What is there to prevent us from riding right across the stream? Have you scattered caltrops amongst the rocks? Hidden archers in the ashes? Tell me what you think is going to stop us."
247	
248	"Me." He pulled one gauntlet off. "In Flea Bottom I was always bigger and stronger than the other boys, so I used to beat them bloody and steal from them. The old man taught me not to do that. It was wrong, he said, and besides, sometimes little boys have great big brothers. Here, have a look at this." Dunk twisted the ring off his finger and held it out to her. She had to let loose of her braid to take it.
249	
250	"Gold?" she said, when she felt the weight of it. "What is this, ser?" She turned it over in her hand. "A signet. Gold and onyx." Her green eyes narrowed as she studied the seal. "Where did you find this, ser?"
251	
252	"In a boot. Wrapped in rags and stuffed up in the toe."
253	
254	Lady Rohanne's fingers closed around it. She glanced at Egg and old Ser Eustace. "You took a great risk in showing me this ring, ser. But how does it avail us? If I should command my men to cross . . ."
255	
256	"Well," said Dunk, "that would mean I'd have to fight."
257	
258	"And die."
259	
260	"Most like," he said, "and then Egg would go back where he comes from, and tell what happened here."
261	
262	"Not if he died as well."
263	
264	"I don't think you'd kill a boy of ten," he said, hoping he was right. "Not this boy of ten, you wouldn't. You've got three-and-thirty men there, like you said. Men talk. That fat one there especially. No matter how deep you dug the graves, the tale would out. And then, well . . . might be a spotted spider's bite can kill a lion, but a dragon is a different sort of beast."
265	
266	"I would sooner be the dragon's friend." She tried the ring on her finger. It was too big even for her thumb. "Dragon or no, I must have Bennis of the Brown Shield."
267	
268	"No."
269	
270	"You are seven feet of stubborn."
271	
272	"Less an inch."
273	
274	She gave him back the ring. "I cannot return to Coldmoat empty-handed. They will say the Red Widow has lost her bite, that she was too weak to do justice, that she could not protect her smallfolk. You do not understand, ser."
275	
276	"I might." Better than you know. "I remember once some little lord in the stormlands took Ser Arlan into service, to help him fight some other little lord. When I asked the old man what they were fighting over, he said, Nothing, lad. It's just some pissing contest.' "
277	
278	Lady Rohanne gave him a shocked look, but could sustain it no more than half a heartbeat before it turned into a grin. "I have heard a thousand empty courtesies in my time, but you are the first knight who ever said pissing in my presence." Her freckled face went somber. "Those pissing contests are how lords judge one another's strength, and woe to any man who shows his weakness. A woman must needs piss twice as hard, if she hopes to rule. And if that woman should happen to be small . . . Lord Stackhouse covets my Horseshoe Hills, Ser Clifford Conklyn has an old claim to Leafy Lake, those dismal Durwells live by stealing cattle . . . and beneath mine own roof I have the Longinch. Every day I wake wondering if this might be the day he marries me by force." Her hand curled tight around her braid, as hard as if it were a rope, and she was dangling over a precipice. "He wants to, I know. He holds back for fear of my wroth, just as Conklyn and Stackhouse and the Durwells tread carefully where the Red Widow is concerned. If any of them thought for a moment that I had turned weak and soft . . ."
279	
280	Dunk put the ring back on his finger, and drew his dagger.
281	
282	The widow's eyes went wide at the sight of naked steel. "What are you doing?" she said. "Have you lost your wits ? There are a dozen crossbows trained on you."
283	
284	"You wanted blood for blood." He laid the dagger against his cheek. "They told you wrong. It wasn't Bennis cut that digger, it was me." He pressed the edge of the steel into his face, slashed downward.
285	
286	When he shook the blood off the blade, some spattered on her face. More freckles, he thought. "There, the Red Widow has her due. A cheek for a cheek."
287	
288	"You are quite mad." The smoke had filled her eyes with tears. "If you were better born, I'd marry you."
289	
290	"Aye, m'lady. And if pigs had wings and scales and breathed flame, they'd be as good as dragons."
291	
292	Dunk slid the knife back in its sheath. His face had begun to throb. The blood ran down his cheek and dripped onto his gorget. The smell made Thunder snort, and paw the water. "Give me the men who burned the wood."
293	
294	"No one burned the wood," she said, "but if some man of mine had done so, it must have been to please me. How could I give such a man to you?" She glanced back at her escort. "It would be best if Ser Eustace were just to withdraw his accusation."
295	
296	"Those pigs will be breathing fire first, m'lady."
297	
298	"In that case, I must assert my innocence before the eyes of gods and men. Tell Ser Eustace that I demand an apology . . . or a trial. The choice is his." She wheeled her horse about to ride back to her men.
299

file.content:
---

real_identity: Duncan the Tall
pov_chapter_number: 1
pov_label: "Dunk I"
collection: tales-of-dunk-and-egg
first_available: pre-agot
file_name: tss-dunk-01-p07.md
unit_part: "7 of 8"
part_of: tss-dunk-01
scene_label: "Fire alarm, Widow's army arrives, stream parley"
---

That last night Dunk wanted to be as far from Eustace Osgrey as he could, so they slept down in the cellar, amongst the rest of Standfast's meager host. It was a restless night. Lem and red-eyed Pate both snored, the one loudly and the other constantly. Dank vapors filled the cellar, rising through the trap from the deeper vaults below. Dunk tossed and turned on the scratchy bed, drifting off into a half sleep only to wake suddenly in darkness. The bites he'd gotten in the woods were itching fiercely, and there were fleas in the straw as well. I will be well rid of this place, well rid of the old man, and Ser Bennis, and the rest of them. Maybe it was time that he took Egg back to Summerhall to see his father. He would ask the boy about that in the morning, when they were well away.

Morning seemed a long way off, though. Dunk's head was full of dragons, red and black . . . full of chequy lions, old shields, battered boots . . . full of streams and moats and dams, and papers stamped with the king's great seal that he could not read.

And she was there as well, the Red Widow, Rohanne of the Coldmoat. He could see her freckled face, her slender arms, her long red braid. It made him feel guilty. I should be dreaming of Tanselle. Tanselle Too-Tall, they called her, but she was not too tall for me. She had painted arms upon his shield and he had saved her from the Bright Prince, but she vanished even before the trial of seven. She could not bear to see me die, Dunk often told himself, but what did he know? He was as thick as a castle wall.

Just thinking of the Red Widow was proof enough of that. Tanselle smiled at me, but we never held each other, never kissed, not even lips to cheek. Rohanne at least had touched him; he had the swollen lip to prove it. Don't be daft. She's not for the likes of you. She is too small, too clever, and much too dangerous.

Drowsing at long last, Dunk dreamed. He was running through a glade in the heart of Wat's Wood, running toward Rohanne, and she was shooting arrows at him. Each shaft she loosed flew true, and pierced him through the chest, yet the pain was strangely sweet. He should have turned and fled, but he ran toward her instead, running slowly as you always did in dreams, as if the very air had turned to honey. Another arrow came, and yet another. Her quiver seemed to have no end of shafts. Her eyes were gray and green and full of mischief. Your gown brings out the color of your eyes, he meant to say to her, but she was not wearing any gown, or any clothes at all. Across her small breasts was a faint spray of freckles, and her nipples were red and hard as little berries. The arrows made him look like some great porcupine as he went stumbling to her feet, but somehow he still found the strength to grab her braid. With one hard yank he pulled her down on top of him and kissed her.

He woke suddenly, at the sound of a shout.

In the darkened cellar, all was confusion. Curses and complaints echoed back and forth, and men were stumbling over one another as they fumbled for their spears or breeches. No one knew what was happening. Egg found the tallow candle and got it lit, to shed some light upon the scene. Dunk was the first one up the steps. He almost collided with Sam Stoops rushing down, puffing like a bellows and babbling incoherently. Dunk had to hold him by both shoulders to keep him from falling. "Sam, what's wrong?"

"The sky," the old man whimpered. "The sky !" No more sense could be gotten from him, so they all went up to the roof for a look. Ser Eustace was there before them, standing by the parapets in his bedrobe, staring off into the distance.

The sun was rising in the west.

It was a long moment before Dunk realized what that meant. "Wat's Wood is afire," he said in a hushed voice. From down at the base of the tower came the sound of Bennis cursing, a stream of such surpassing filth that it might have made Aegon the Unworthy blush. Sam Stoops began to pray.

They were too far away to make out flames, but the red glow engulfed half the western horizon, and above the light the stars were vanishing. The King's Crown was half gone already, obscured behind a veil of the rising smoke.

Fire and sword, she said.

The fire burned until morning. No one in Standfast slept that night. Before long they could smell the smoke, and see flames dancing in the distance like girls in scarlet skirts. They all wondered if the fire would engulf them. Dunk stood behind the parapets, his eyes burning, watching for riders in the night.

"Bennis," he said, when the brown knight came up, chewing on his sourleaf, "it's you she wants. Might be you should go."

"What, run?" he brayed. "On my horse? Might as well try to fly off on one o' these damned chickens."

"Then give yourself up. She'll only slit your nose."

"I like my nose how it is, lunk. Let her try and take me, we'll see what gets slit open." He sat cross-legged with his back against a merlon and took a whetstone from his pouch to sharpen his sword.

Ser Eustace stood above him. In low voices, they spoke of how to fight the war. "The Longinch will expect us at the dam," Dunk heard the old knight say, "so we will burn her crops instead. Fire for fire."

Ser Bennis thought that would be just the thing, only maybe they should put her mill to the torch as well.

"It's six leagues on t'other side o' the castle, the Longinch won't be looking for us there. Burn the mill and kill the miller, that'll cost her dear."

Egg was listening, too. He coughed, and looked at Dunk with wide white eyes. "Ser, you have to stop them."

"How?" Dunk asked. The Red Widow will stop them. Her, and that Lucas the Longinch. "They're only making noise, Egg. It's that, or piss their breeches. And it's naught to do with us now."

Dawn came with hazy gray skies and air that burned the eyes. Dunk meant to make an early start, though after their sleepless night he did not know how far they'd get. He and Egg broke their fast on boiled eggs while Bennis was rousting the others outside for more drill. They are Osgrey men and we are not, he told himself. He ate four of the eggs. Ser Eustace owed him that much, as he saw it. Egg ate two. They washed them down with ale.

"We could go to Fair Isle, ser," the boy said as they were gathering up their things. "If they're being raided by the ironmen, Lord Farman might be looking for some swords."

It was a good thought. "Have you ever been to Fair Isle?"

"No, ser," Egg said, "but they say it's fair. Lord Farman's seat is fair, too. It's called Faircastle."

Dunk laughed. "Faircastle it shall be." He felt as if a great weight had been lifted off his shoulders. "I'll see to the horses," he said, when he'd tied his armor up in a bundle, secured with hempen rope. "Go to the roof and get our bedrolls, squire." The last thing he wanted this morning was another confrontation with the chequy lion. "If you see Ser Eustace, let him be."

"I will, ser."

Outside, Bennis had his recruits lined up with their spears and shields, and was trying to teach them to advance in unison. The brown knight paid Dunk not the slightest heed as he crossed the yard. He will lead the whole lot of them to death. The Red Widow could be here any moment. Egg came bursting from the tower door and clattered down the wooden steps with their bedrolls. Above him, Ser Eustace stood stiffly on the balcony, his hands resting on the parapet. When his eyes met Dunk's his mustache quivered, and he quickly turned away. The air was hazy with blowing smoke.

Bennis had his shield slung across his back, a tall kite shield of unpainted wood, dark with countless layers of old varnish and girded all about with iron. It bore no blazon, only a center bosse that reminded Dunk of some great eye, shut tight. As blind as he is. "How do you mean to fight her?" Dunk asked.

Ser Bennis looked at his soldiers, his mouth running red with sourleaf. "Can't hold the hill with so few spears. Got to be the tower. We all hole up inside." He nodded at the door. "Only one way in. Haul up them wooden steps, and there's no way they can reach us."

"Until they build some steps of their own. They might bring ropes and grapnels, too, and swarm down on you through the roof. Unless they just stand back with their crossbows and fill you full of quarrels while you're trying to hold the door."

The Melons, Beans, and Barleycorns were listening to all they said. All their brave talk had blown away, though there was no breath of wind. They stood clutching their sharpened sticks, looking at Dunk and Bennis and each other.

"This lot won't do you a lick of good," Dunk said, with a nod at the ragged Osgrey army. "The Red Widow's knights will cut them to pieces if you leave them in the open, and their spears won't be any use inside that tower."

"They can chuck things off the roof," said Bennis. "Treb is good at chucking rocks."

"He could chuck a rock or two, I suppose," said Dunk, "until one of the Widow's crossbowmen puts a bolt through him."

"Ser?" Egg stood beside him. "Ser, if we mean to go, we'd best be gone, in case the Widow comes."

The boy was right. If we linger, we'll be trapped here. Yet still Dunk hesitated. "Let them go, Bennis."

"What, lose our valiant lads?" Bennis looked at the peasants, and brayed laughter. "Don't you lot be getting any notions," he warned them. "I'll gut any man who tries to run."

"Try, and I'll gut you." Dunk drew his sword. "Go home, all of you," he told the smallfolk. "Go back to your villages, and see if the fire's spared your homes and crops."

No one moved. The brown knight stared at him, his mouth working. Dunk ignored him. "Go," he told the smallfolk once again. It was as if some god had put the word into his mouth. Not the Warrior. Is there a god for fools? "GO!" he said again, roaring it this time. "Take your spears and shields, but go , or you won't live to see the morrow. Do you want to kiss your wives again? Do you want to hold your children? Go home! Have you all gone deaf?"

They hadn't. A mad scramble ensued amongst the chickens. Big Rob trod on a hen as he made his dash, and Pate came within half a foot of disemboweling Will Bean when his own spear tripped him up, but off they went, running. The Melons went one way, the Beans another, the Barleycorns a third. Ser Eustace was shouting down at them from above, but no one paid him any mind. They are deaf to him at least, Dunk thought.

By the time the old knight emerged from his tower and came scrambling down the steps, only Dunk and Egg and Bennis remained among the chickens. "Come back," Ser Eustace shouted at his fast-fleeing host. "You do not have my leave to go. You do not have my leave!"

"No use, m'lord," said Bennis. "They're gone."

Ser Eustace rounded on Dunk, his mustache quivering with rage. "You had no right to send them away. No right! I told them not to go, I forbade it. I forbade you to dismiss them."

"We never heard you, my lord." Egg took off his hat to fan away the smoke. "The chickens were cackling too loud."

The old man sank down onto Standfast's lowest step. "What did that woman offer you to deliver me to her?" he asked Dunk in a bleak voice. "How much gold did she give you to betray me, to send my lads away and leave me here alone?"

"You're not alone, m'lord." Dunk sheathed his sword. "I slept beneath your roof, and ate your eggs this morning. I owe you some service still. I won't go slinking off with my tail between my legs. My sword's still here." He touched the hilt.

"One sword." The old knight got slowly to his feet. "What can one sword hope to do against that woman?"

"Try and keep her off your land, to start with." Dunk wished he were as certain as he sounded.

The old knight's mustache trembled every time he took a breath. "Yes," he said at last. "Better to go boldly than hide behind stone walls. Better to die a lion than a rabbit. We were the Marshalls of the Northmarch for a thousand years. I must have my armor." He started up the steps.

Egg was looking up at Dunk. "I never knew you had a tail, ser," the boy said.

"Do you want a clout in the ear?"

"No, ser. Do you want your armor?"

"That," Dunk said, "and one thing more."

There was talk of Ser Bennis coming with them, but in the end Ser Eustace commanded him to stay and hold the tower. His sword would be of little use against the odds that they were like to face, and the sight of him would inflame the Widow further.

The brown knight did not require much convincing. Dunk helped him knock loose the iron pegs that held the upper steps in place. Bennis clambered up them, untied the old gray hempen rope, and hauled on it with all his strength. Creaking and groaning, the wooden stair swung upward, leaving ten feet of air between the top stone step and the tower's only entrance. Sam Stoops and his wife were both inside.

The chickens would need to fend for themselves. Sitting below on his gray gelding, Ser Eustace called up to say, "If we have not returned by nightfall . . ."

". . . I'll ride for Highgarden, m'lord, and tell Lord Tyrell how that woman burned your wood and murdered you."

Dunk followed Egg and Maester down the hill. The old man came after, his armor rattling softly. For once a wind was rising, and he could hear the flapping of his cloak.

Where Wat's Wood had stood they found a smoking wasteland. The fire had largely burned itself out by the time they reached the wood, but here and there a few patches were still burning, fiery islands in a sea of ash and cinders. Elsewhere the trunks of burned trees thrust like blackened spears into the sky. Other trees had fallen and lay athwart the west way with limbs charred and broken, dull red fires smoldering inside their hollow hearts. There were hot spots on the forest floor as well, and places where the smoke hung in the air like a hot gray haze. Ser Eustace was stricken with a fit of coughing, and for a few moments Dunk feared the old man would need to turn back, but finally it passed.

They rode past the carcass of a red deer, and later on what might have been a badger. Nothing lived, except the flies. Flies could live through anything, it seemed.

"The Field of Fire must have looked like this," Ser Eustace said. "It was there our woes began, two hundred years ago. The last of the green kings perished on that field, with the finest flowers of the Reach around him. My father said the dragonfire burned so hot that their swords melted in their hands.

Afterward the blades were gathered up, and went to make the Iron Throne. Highgarden passed from kings to stewards, and the Osgreys dwindled and diminished, until the Marshalls of the Northmarch were no more than landed knights bound in fealty to the Rowans."

Dunk had nothing to say to that, so they rode in silence for a time, till Ser Eustace coughed, and said,

"Ser Duncan, do you remember the story that I told you?"

"I might, ser," said Dunk. "Which one?"

"The Little Lion."

"I remember. He was the youngest of five sons."

"Good." He coughed again. "When he slew Lancel Lannister, the westermen turned back. Without the king there was no war. Do you understand what I am saying?"

"Aye," Dunk said reluctantly. Could I kill a woman? For once Dunk wished he were as thick as that castle wall. It must not come to that. I must not let it come to that.

A few green trees still stood where the west way crossed the Chequy Water. Their trunks were charred and blackened on one side. Just beyond, the water glimmered darkly. Blue and green, Dunk thought, but all the gold is gone. The smoke had veiled the sun.

Ser Eustace halted when he reached the water's edge. "I took a holy vow. I will not cross that stream. Not so long as the land beyond is hers ." The old knight wore mail and plate beneath his yellowed surcoat. His sword was on his hip.

"What if she never comes, ser?" Egg asked.

With fire and sword, Dunk thought. "She'll come."

She did, and within the hour. They heard her horses first, and then the faint metallic sound of clinking armor, growing louder. The drifting smoke made it hard to tell how far off they were, until her banner bearer pushed through the ragged gray curtain. His staff was crowned by an iron spider painted white and red, with the black banner of the Webbers hanging listlessly beneath. When he saw them across the water, he halted on the bank. Ser Lucas Inchfield appeared half a heartbeat later, armored head to heel.

Only then did Lady Rohanne herself appear, astride a coal-black mare decked out in strands of silverly silk, like unto a spider's web. The Widow's cloak was made of the same stuff. It billowed from her shoulders and her wrists, as light as air. She was armored, too, in a suit of green enamel scale chased with gold and silver. It fit her figure like a glove, and made her look as if she were garbed in summer leaves. Her long red braid hung down behind her, bouncing as she rode. Septon Sefton rode red-faced at her side, atop a big gray gelding. On her other side was her young maester, Cerrick, mounted on a mule.

More knights came after, half a dozen of them, attended by as many esquires. A column of mounted crossbowmen brought up the rear, and fanned out to either side of the road when they reached the Chequy Water and saw Dunk waiting on the other side. There were three-and-thirty fighting men all told, excluding the septon, the maester, and the Widow herself. One of the knights caught Dunk's eye; a squat bald keg of a man in mail and leather, with an angry face and an ugly goiter on his neck.

The Red Widow walked her mare to the edge of the water. "Ser Eustace, Ser Duncan," she called across the stream, "we saw your fire burning in the night."

"Saw it?" Ser Eustace shouted back. "Aye, you saw it . . . after you made it."

"That is a vile accusation."

"For a vile act."

"I was asleep in my bed last night, with my ladies all around me. The shouts from the walls awoke me, as they did almost everyone. Old men climbed up steep tower steps to look, and babes at the breast saw the red light and wept in fear. And that is all I know of your fire, ser."

"It was your fire, woman," insisted Ser Eustace. "My wood is gone. Gone, I say!"

Septon Sefton cleared his throat. "Ser Eustace," he boomed, "there are fires in the kingswood too, and even in the rainwood. The drought has turned all our woods to kindling."

Lady Rohanne raised an arm and pointed. "Look at my fields, Osgrey. How dry they are. I would have been a fool to set a fire. Had the wind changed direction, the flames might well have leapt the stream, and burned out half my crops."

"Might have?" Ser Eustace shouted. "It was my woods that burned, and you that burned them. Most like you cast some witch's spell to drive the wind, just as you used your dark arts to slay your husbands and your brothers!"

Lady Rohanne's face grew harder. Dunk had seen that look at Coldmoat, just before she slapped him.

"Prattle," she told the old man. "I will waste no more words on you, ser. Produce Bennis of the Brown Shield, or we will come and take him."

"That you shall not do," Ser Eustace declared in ringing tones. "That you shall never do." His mustache twitched. "Come no farther. This side of the stream is mine, and you are not wanted here. You shall have no hospitality from me. No bread and salt, not even shade and water. You come as an intruder. I forbid you to set foot on Osgrey land."

Lady Rohanne drew her braid over her shoulder. "Ser Lucas," was all she said. The Longinch made a gesture, the crossbowmen dismounted, winched back their bowstrings with the help of hook and stirrup, and plucked quarrels from their quivers. "Now, ser," her ladyship called out, when every bow was nocked and raised and ready, "what was it you forbade me?"

Dunk had heard enough. "If you cross the stream without leave, you are breaking the king's peace."

Septon Sefton urged his horse forward a step. "The king will neither know nor care," he called. "We are all the Mother's children, ser. For her sake, stand aside."

Dunk frowned. "I don't know much of gods, septon . . . but aren't we the Warrior's children, too?" He rubbed the back of his neck. "If you try to cross, I'll stop you."

Ser Lucas the Longinch laughed. "Here's a hedge knight who yearns to be a hedgehog, my lady," he said to the Red Widow. "Say the word, and we'll put a dozen quarrels in him. At this distance they will punch through that armor like it was made of spit."

"No. Not yet, ser." Lady Rohanne studied him from across the stream. "You are two men and a boy. We are three-and-thirty. How do you propose to stop us crossing?"

"Well," said Dunk, "I'll tell you. But only you."

"As you wish." She pressed her heels into her horse and rode her out into the stream. When the water reached the mare's belly, she halted, waiting. "Here I am. Come closer, ser. I promise not to sew you in a sack."

Ser Eustace grasped Dunk by the arm before he could respond. "Go to her," the old knight said, "but remember the Little Lion."

"As you say, m'lord." Dunk walked Thunder down into the water. He drew up beside her and said,

"M'lady."

"Ser Duncan." She reached up and laid two fingers on his swollen lip. "Did I do this, ser?"

"No one else has slapped my face of late, m'lady."

"That was bad of me. A breach of hospitality. The good septon has been scolding me." She gazed across the water at Ser Eustace. "I scarce remember Addam any longer. It was more than half my life ago. I remember that I loved him, though. I have not loved any of the others."

"His father put him in the blackberries, with his brothers," Dunk said. "He was fond of blackberries."

"I remember. He used to pick them for me, and we'd eat them in a bowl of cream."

"The king pardoned the old man for Daemon," said Dunk. "It is past time you pardoned him for Addam."

"Give me Bennis, and I'll consider that."

"Bennis is not mine to give."

She sighed. "I would as like not have to kill you."

"I would as like not die."

"Then give me Bennis. We'll cut his nose off and hand him back, and that will be the end of that."

"It won't, though," Dunk said. "There's still the dam to deal with, and the fire. Will you give us the men who set it?"

"There were lantern bugs in that wood," she said. "It may be they set the fire off, with their little lanterns."

"No more teasing now, m'lady," Dunk warned her. "This is no time for it. Tear down the dam, and let Ser Eustace have the water to make up for the wood. That's fair, is it not?"

"It might be, if I had burned the wood. Which I did not. I was at Coldmoat, safe abed." She looked down at the water. "What is there to prevent us from riding right across the stream? Have you scattered caltrops amongst the rocks? Hidden archers in the ashes? Tell me what you think is going to stop us."

"Me." He pulled one gauntlet off. "In Flea Bottom I was always bigger and stronger than the other boys, so I used to beat them bloody and steal from them. The old man taught me not to do that. It was wrong, he said, and besides, sometimes little boys have great big brothers. Here, have a look at this." Dunk twisted the ring off his finger and held it out to her. She had to let loose of her braid to take it.

"Gold?" she said, when she felt the weight of it. "What is this, ser?" She turned it over in her hand. "A signet. Gold and onyx." Her green eyes narrowed as she studied the seal. "Where did you find this, ser?"

"In a boot. Wrapped in rags and stuffed up in the toe."

Lady Rohanne's fingers closed around it. She glanced at Egg and old Ser Eustace. "You took a great risk in showing me this ring, ser. But how does it avail us? If I should command my men to cross . . ."

"Well," said Dunk, "that would mean I'd have to fight."

"And die."

"Most like," he said, "and then Egg would go back where he comes from, and tell what happened here."

"Not if he died as well."

"I don't think you'd kill a boy of ten," he said, hoping he was right. "Not this boy of ten, you wouldn't. You've got three-and-thirty men there, like you said. Men talk. That fat one there especially. No matter how deep you dug the graves, the tale would out. And then, well . . . might be a spotted spider's bite can kill a lion, but a dragon is a different sort of beast."

"I would sooner be the dragon's friend." She tried the ring on her finger. It was too big even for her thumb. "Dragon or no, I must have Bennis of the Brown Shield."

"No."

"You are seven feet of stubborn."

"Less an inch."

She gave him back the ring. "I cannot return to Coldmoat empty-handed. They will say the Red Widow has lost her bite, that she was too weak to do justice, that she could not protect her smallfolk. You do not understand, ser."

"I might." Better than you know. "I remember once some little lord in the stormlands took Ser Arlan into service, to help him fight some other little lord. When I asked the old man what they were fighting over, he said, Nothing, lad. It's just some pissing contest.' "

Lady Rohanne gave him a shocked look, but could sustain it no more than half a heartbeat before it turned into a grin. "I have heard a thousand empty courtesies in my time, but you are the first knight who ever said pissing in my presence." Her freckled face went somber. "Those pissing contests are how lords judge one another's strength, and woe to any man who shows his weakness. A woman must needs piss twice as hard, if she hopes to rule. And if that woman should happen to be small . . . Lord Stackhouse covets my Horseshoe Hills, Ser Clifford Conklyn has an old claim to Leafy Lake, those dismal Durwells live by stealing cattle . . . and beneath mine own roof I have the Longinch. Every day I wake wondering if this might be the day he marries me by force." Her hand curled tight around her braid, as hard as if it were a rope, and she was dangling over a precipice. "He wants to, I know. He holds back for fear of my wroth, just as Conklyn and Stackhouse and the Durwells tread carefully where the Red Widow is concerned. If any of them thought for a moment that I had turned weak and soft . . ."

Dunk put the ring back on his finger, and drew his dagger.

The widow's eyes went wide at the sight of naked steel. "What are you doing?" she said. "Have you lost your wits ? There are a dozen crossbows trained on you."

"You wanted blood for blood." He laid the dagger against his cheek. "They told you wrong. It wasn't Bennis cut that digger, it was me." He pressed the edge of the steel into his face, slashed downward.

When he shook the blood off the blade, some spattered on her face. More freckles, he thought. "There, the Red Widow has her due. A cheek for a cheek."

"You are quite mad." The smoke had filled her eyes with tears. "If you were better born, I'd marry you."

"Aye, m'lady. And if pigs had wings and scales and breathed flame, they'd be as good as dragons."

Dunk slid the knife back in its sheath. His face had begun to throb. The blood ran down his cheek and dripped onto his gorget. The smell made Thunder snort, and paw the water. "Give me the men who burned the wood."

"No one burned the wood," she said, "but if some man of mine had done so, it must have been to please me. How could I give such a man to you?" She glanced back at her escort. "It would be best if Ser Eustace were just to withdraw his accusation."

"Those pigs will be breathing fire first, m'lady."

"In that case, I must assert my innocence before the eyes of gods and men. Tell Ser Eustace that I demand an apology . . . or a trial. The choice is his." She wheeled her horse about to ride back to her men.

1	# TSS — Dunk 1

4	- **book:** TSS

6	- **pov_character:** Dunk
7	- **pov_chapter_number:** Dunk I
8	- **first_available:** pre-agot
9	- **location_primary:** Standfast (tower) and the road/woods between Coldmoat and Standfast
10	- **location_secondary:** Wat's Wood, the Chequy Water and its dam, Coldmoat (departed at the opening)
11	- **approximate_timeline:** A single evening/night — Dunk and Egg ride home from Coldmoat, detour to the dam, cross Wat's Wood after dark, and reach Standfast by moonrise, where Dunk resigns Ser Eustace's service.
12	- **time_markers:** "Not until the walls and towers of Coldmoat had vanished in the west"; "Night caught them in Wat's Wood"; "the sun was low on the horizon and the first stars were coming out, along with clouds of mites"; "The gloom was deepening"; "The moon had risen by the time they came to Standfast"; "a thousand years" (Osgreys as Marshalls; Manderlys driven off); "He has been at Summerhall for a year, and half of another"; "Alysanne was seven when they took her off … and twenty when she died"; "a boy of twelve" (Daemon knighted); "back before the Conquest" / "since the Conquest" (the silver cup, Blackfyre); "It was all years ago" (the rebellion)
13	- **chapter_summary:** Riding home from Coldmoat, Egg recounts the royal grant that gives the Chequy Water to Lord Webber and would revert Standfast to the crown should Ser Eustace die without a male heir. The pair detour to the dam, find it guarded by Webber crossbowmen, and retreat through Wat's Wood after dark, debating bastardy, Lord Bloodraven, and Dunk's unknown parentage. At Standfast, Ser Eustace confesses that his sons died fighting for Daemon Blackfyre, the "black dragon," not for King Daeron, and that he bought his pardon by giving his daughter Alysanne to King's Landing as a hostage. When Eustace insults Egg's father and threatens to beat the boy, Dunk resigns his service, lays his cloak in the old knight's lap, and is cast out.
14	- **unit_part:** 6 of 8
15	
16	## Physical Environment
17	- **Weather:** Clear enough to see stars overhead, but "there were clouds to the north" that hid the Ice Dragon's blue eye.
18	- **Season indicators:** "clouds of mites"; "Lantern bugs moved slowly through the trees"; blackberries/blackberry graves referenced (recalled).
19	- **Time of day:** Late afternoon into full night — sun low, first stars, then moonrise.
20	- **Lighting:** Setting sun; first stars and "more stars than any man could ever hope to count"; lantern bugs "like so many drifting stars"; the risen moon; "A pale yellow light … spilling from the tower's upper windows"; Bennis honing his sword "in the moonlight"; Eustace's chamber with "no fire had been laid."
21	- **Sounds:** "The slow scrape of stone on steel carried a long way" (Bennis honing his longsword).
22	- **Smells:** —
23	- **Notable sensory details:** "Amongst the tall black trees"; the stream they "splashed across"; the moon on Standfast "standing dark and tall atop its hill."

29	| Egg | Dunk's squire; reads the grant, debates Eustace | No | Son of Prince Maekar; served the king's small council at court |
30	| Ser Bennis of the Brown Shield | Garrison man-at-arms, keeping watch at Standfast | No | Chewing sourleaf, honing his longsword |
31	| Ser Eustace Osgrey | Old knight, Dunk's (former) master; reveals his black-dragon past | No | Half drunk, in his bedrobe, holding his father's silver cup |

148	## Events & Actions
149	1. **Egg recounts the grant of rights** — As Coldmoat vanishes behind them, Egg recites the sealed grant from memory: the Chequy Water and hunting/wood rights in Wat's Wood go to Lord Wyman Webber, and Standfast reverts to the crown if Eustace dies without a male heir.
150	   - Agent: Egg
151	   - Patient: Dunk (audience)
152	   - Location: The road west of Coldmoat
153	   - Outcome: Information conveyed
154	2. **Detour to the dam** — Dunk leads them north for another look at the dam, half-meaning to tear it apart.
155	   - Agent: Dunk
156	   - Location: Wat's Wood / the Chequy Water
157	   - Outcome: Detour
158	3. **The dam is found guarded; Dunk retreats** — Two Webber crossbowmen with spider badges guard the dam, one barefoot in the stream; both ready their bows, and Dunk can only "scowl at them threateningly" before retracing his steps.
159	   - Agent: The two crossbowmen
160	   - Patient: Dunk and Egg
161	   - Instrument: Crossbows
162	   - Location: The dam
163	   - Outcome: Dunk forced to withdraw
164	4. **Night crossing of Wat's Wood** — They splash back across the stream at sundown and travel on through the dark wood, debating bastardy and Bloodraven.
165	   - Agent: Dunk and Egg
166	   - Location: Wat's Wood
167	   - Outcome: Arrival at Standfast by moonrise
168	5. **Bennis reports at Standfast** — Bennis, keeping watch on the steps, tells Dunk the men are abed or on the roof (Treb and Wet Wat) and that he worked the garrison hard, bloodying "that big lackwit."
169	   - Agent: Bennis
170	   - Patient: Dunk (audience)
171	   - Location: Standfast steps
172	   - Outcome: Status conveyed
173	6. **Egg tends the mounts** — At Dunk's order, Egg goes to "Tend to Thunder and Maester" before joining them upstairs.
174	   - Agent: Egg
175	   - Instigator: Dunk
176	   - Location: Standfast stable
177	7. **Dunk reports the parley to Eustace** — Dunk relays the Red Widow's terms (she keeps the water, wants Bennis, holds a sealed royal parchment), omitting the Lady Helicent episode; he admits she slapped him.
178	   - Agent: Dunk
179	   - Patient: Ser Eustace (audience)
180	   - Location: Eustace's chamber
181	   - Outcome: Eustace outraged at the slap
182	8. **Ser Eustace confesses his black-dragon loyalty** — Eustace admits his sons died for Daemon Blackfyre, "the rightful king," not for Daeron; that he marched to seat Daemon on the Iron Throne; and lists the "ifs" by which the rebellion might have been won.
183	   - Agent: Ser Eustace
184	   - Patient: Dunk (audience)
185	   - Location: Eustace's chamber
186	   - Witness: Dunk
187	   - Outcome: The concealed loyalty revealed
188	9. **Eustace recounts the hostage price** — Eustace tells how he bought his pardon by surrendering his seven-year-old daughter Alysanne, who died a silent sister at twenty and refused to speak to him.
189	   - Agent: Ser Eustace
190	   - Patient: Dunk (audience)
191	   - Location: Eustace's chamber
192	   - Outcome: The daughter's fate revealed
193	10. **Egg challenges Eustace; Eustace insults Maekar** — Egg presses why Eustace chose Daemon and quotes his father; Eustace calls the boy's father "a fool," and, when Egg objects, threatens to beat him.
194	    - Agent: Ser Eustace
195	    - Patient: Egg
196	    - Location: Eustace's chamber
197	    - Outcome: Confrontation escalates
198	11. **Dunk resigns Ser Eustace's service** — Dunk refuses to let Eustace beat Egg, declares they will leave at first light, and lays his rolled cloak in the old knight's lap.
199	    - Agent: Dunk
200	    - Patient: Ser Eustace
201	    - Instrument: His cloak (laid in the lap)
202	    - Location: Eustace's chamber
203	    - Outcome: Service ended
204	12. **Eustace casts them out** — Eustace accuses Dunk of leaving for the Red Widow, then throws them out: "Go, then. Go. I want none of you."
205	    - Agent: Ser Eustace
206	    - Patient: Dunk and Egg
207	    - Location: Eustace's chamber
208	    - Outcome: Expulsion

256	## POV Character's Internal State
257	- **Emotional state:** Betrayed and disillusioned — anger at being "played for a fool," pity for the old knight, unsettled reflection on his own origins.
258	- **Primary preoccupation:** That Eustace lied about his sons and the stream; whether the old knight's cause and honor were real; and, prompted by the bastardy talk, his own unknown parentage.
259	- **Key decisions made:** To resign Ser Eustace's service and leave Standfast at first light, refusing to let Eustace beat Egg.
260	- **Self-deception flags:** "Dunk thought he knew the answer, but it was not one the boy would want to hear"; he omits the Lady Helicent episode from his report because it "made him look an utter fool."

# TSS — Dunk 1

## Chapter Metadata
- **book:** TSS
- **chapter_number:** 1
- **pov_character:** Dunk
- **pov_chapter_number:** Dunk I
- **first_available:** pre-agot
- **location_primary:** Standfast (tower) and the road/woods between Coldmoat and Standfast
- **location_secondary:** Wat's Wood, the Chequy Water and its dam, Coldmoat (departed at the opening)
- **approximate_timeline:** A single evening/night — Dunk and Egg ride home from Coldmoat, detour to the dam, cross Wat's Wood after dark, and reach Standfast by moonrise, where Dunk resigns Ser Eustace's service.
- **time_markers:** "Not until the walls and towers of Coldmoat had vanished in the west"; "Night caught them in Wat's Wood"; "the sun was low on the horizon and the first stars were coming out, along with clouds of mites"; "The gloom was deepening"; "The moon had risen by the time they came to Standfast"; "a thousand years" (Osgreys as Marshalls; Manderlys driven off); "He has been at Summerhall for a year, and half of another"; "Alysanne was seven when they took her off … and twenty when she died"; "a boy of twelve" (Daemon knighted); "back before the Conquest" / "since the Conquest" (the silver cup, Blackfyre); "It was all years ago" (the rebellion)
- **chapter_summary:** Riding home from Coldmoat, Egg recounts the royal grant that gives the Chequy Water to Lord Webber and would revert Standfast to the crown should Ser Eustace die without a male heir. The pair detour to the dam, find it guarded by Webber crossbowmen, and retreat through Wat's Wood after dark, debating bastardy, Lord Bloodraven, and Dunk's unknown parentage. At Standfast, Ser Eustace confesses that his sons died fighting for Daemon Blackfyre, the "black dragon," not for King Daeron, and that he bought his pardon by giving his daughter Alysanne to King's Landing as a hostage. When Eustace insults Egg's father and threatens to beat the boy, Dunk resigns his service, lays his cloak in the old knight's lap, and is cast out.
- **unit_part:** 6 of 8

## Physical Environment
- **Weather:** Clear enough to see stars overhead, but "there were clouds to the north" that hid the Ice Dragon's blue eye.
- **Season indicators:** "clouds of mites"; "Lantern bugs moved slowly through the trees"; blackberries/blackberry graves referenced (recalled).
- **Time of day:** Late afternoon into full night — sun low, first stars, then moonrise.
- **Lighting:** Setting sun; first stars and "more stars than any man could ever hope to count"; lantern bugs "like so many drifting stars"; the risen moon; "A pale yellow light … spilling from the tower's upper windows"; Bennis honing his sword "in the moonlight"; Eustace's chamber with "no fire had been laid."
- **Sounds:** "The slow scrape of stone on steel carried a long way" (Bennis honing his longsword).
- **Smells:** —
- **Notable sensory details:** "Amongst the tall black trees"; the stream they "splashed across"; the moon on Standfast "standing dark and tall atop its hill."

## Characters Present
| Character | Role in Chapter | First Appearance? | Notes |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|-------|
| Dunk (Ser Duncan the Tall) | POV; hedge knight resigning Eustace's service | No | Swollen split lip from the Red Widow's slap |
| Egg | Dunk's squire; reads the grant, debates Eustace | No | Son of Prince Maekar; served the king's small council at court |
| Ser Bennis of the Brown Shield | Garrison man-at-arms, keeping watch at Standfast | No | Chewing sourleaf, honing his longsword |
| Ser Eustace Osgrey | Old knight, Dunk's (former) master; reveals his black-dragon past | No | Half drunk, in his bedrobe, holding his father's silver cup |
| Two Webber crossbowmen | Guards at the dam | Yes (uncertain — verify) | Spider badges on jerkins; one sat barefoot in the stream |

## Character Appearances
| Character | Hair | Eyes | Build/Height | Scars/Marks | Clothing/Armor | Weapons Worn | Distinguishing Features | Age (if stated) |
|-----------|------|------|-------------|-------------|----------------|-------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Dunk | — | — | Very large ("born too big") | Split lip swollen "twice its normal size" | Cloak (which he surrenders) | — | — | — |
| Ser Bennis | — | — | — | "brown-and-red smile" (sourleaf-stained teeth) | Neglects "his clothes and person" | Longsword (kept well-honed) | Chews sourleaf | — |
| Ser Eustace Osgrey | — | — | — | Trembling/jerking hand | Bedrobe | — | Mustache that "quivered"; half drunk | — |
| King Daeron (as described by Eustace) | — | — | "spindly and round of shoulder, with a little belly that wobbled when he walked" | — | — | — | — | — |
| Prince Daemon (as described by Eustace) | — | — | "stood straight and proud, and his stomach was flat and hard as an oaken shield" | — | — | Wielded Blackfyre | Peerless with ax, lance, flail, and sword | Knighted "a boy of twelve" |
| Webber crossbowmen | — | — | — | — | Jerkins with "spider badges sewn on" | Crossbows (quarrel nocked) | One "sat with his bare feet in the stolen water" | — |

## Characters Referenced

| Alysanne Osgrey | Eustace's daughter; hostage taken at seven, died a silent sister at twenty | Ser Eustace |
| Ser Eustace's sons | Died fighting for Daemon Blackfyre | Ser Eustace, Dunk |
| Gwayne Corbray | "If Daemon had ridden over Gwayne Corbray" | Ser Eustace |
| Fireball | "if Fireball had not been slain on the eve of battle"; among Daemon's heroes | Ser Eustace |
| Lord(s) Hightower, Tarbeck, Oakheart, Butterwell | Kept "one foot in each camp" instead of lending full strength | Ser Eustace |
| Manfred Lothston | "if Manfred Lothston had proved true instead of treacherous" | Ser Eustace |
| Lord Bracken | Whose sailing with the Myrish crossbowmen was delayed by storms | Ser Eustace |
| Quickfinger | "caught with the stolen dragon's eggs" | Ser Eustace |
| Ulrick Dayne | Wielder of Dawn, named as a lesser swordsman than Daemon | Ser Eustace |
| The Dragonknight | Wielder of Dark Sister, named as a lesser swordsman than Daemon | Ser Eustace |
| Robb Reyne | Among Daemon's "roll of heroes" | Ser Eustace |
| Gareth the Grey | Among Daemon's "roll of heroes" | Ser Eustace |
| Ser Aubrey Ambrose | Among Daemon's "roll of heroes" | Ser Eustace |
| Lord Gormon Peake | Among Daemon's "roll of heroes" | Ser Eustace |
| Black Byren Flowers | Among Daemon's "roll of heroes" | Ser Eustace |
| Redtusk | Among Daemon's "roll of heroes" | Ser Eustace |
| The Young Dragon | Earlier king Daeron was named after ("bore the same name as the Young Dragon") | Ser Eustace |
| Baelor (the feeblest king) | The king after whom Daeron named his son; "the feeblest king who ever sat the Iron Throne" | Ser Eustace |
| Baelor (Daeron's son) | Daeron's son by his Dornish wife, named Baelor | Ser Eustace |
| Daeron's sister | "sold his own sweet sister to the prince of Dorne, though it was Daemon that she loved" | Ser Eustace |
| The prince of Dorne | Daeron's sister was married to him | Ser Eustace |
| Daeron's Dornish wife | "he had taken a Dornishwoman into his bed"; bore him Baelor | Ser Eustace |
| Aegon the Conqueror | Original wielder of Blackfyre | Ser Eustace |
| Lucas Inchfield / Lucas Longinch | Coldmoat man; the dam's overlord-figure; Eustace asks if the sight of Dunk gave him pause | Dunk (internal), Ser Eustace |
| The old High Septon | Counselled Maekar that bastards are "born to betrayal" and never to trust Bloodraven | Egg |
| The fat septon | Said Egg's father "sulks in Summerhall" | Egg |
| The "big lackwit" | A garrison recruit Bennis bloodied in training | Bennis |

## Locations

## Location Descriptions

|----------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------------| ------|-----------|------------------------------|------------------------|
| Standfast | A tower keep; upper windows; entered via a trapdoor | Tower with a hearth-and-bedchamber above reached "up through the trap"; upper windows | Hearth "though no fire had been laid"; Eustace sits by it in a bedrobe | "a tower to die in" | Isolated | "standing dark and tall atop its hill" | "A pale yellow light was spilling from the tower's upper windows" under the risen moon |
| The dam | Guarded by two crossbowmen | A built dam holding back the "stolen water" | — | Small enough to be reached on a detour north | Intact and manned | On the Chequy Water within Wat's Wood | A guard "sat with his bare feet in the stolen water" |

## Artifacts & Objects of Significance

| The grant of rights (sealed parchment) | Royal grant to Lord Wyman Webber for "leal service in the late rebellion"; carries the reversion clause on Standfast; bears "the king's own seal" | No | The Red Widow (shown to Dunk at Coldmoat) |
| Ser Eustace's father's silver cup | "a heavy silver cup … made for some Lord Osgrey back before the Conquest," bowl adorned with a "chequy lion … done in flakes of jade and gold, though some of the jade flakes had gone missing" | Yes (uncertain — verify) | Ser Eustace (in his chamber) |
| Blackfyre | "the sword of Aegon the Conqueror, the blade that every Targaryen king had wielded since the Conquest"; put in Daemon's hand at his knighting at twelve | No | (historical; Daemon's) |
| Dawn | The sword borne by Ulrick Dayne | No | (historical; Dayne) |
